news_summary: Recent discoveries reveal important connections between gum health and heart valve risks, new insights into the brain's multitasking abilities, a surprising role of tau protein in memory related to Alzheimer's, and accessible protection through the free NHS meningitis B vaccine for new university students.
news_summary_fr: Des découvertes récentes mettent en évidence des liens importants entre la santé gingivale et les risques liés aux valvules cardiaques, apportent un nouvel éclairage sur les capacités du cerveau à effectuer plusieurs tâches à la fois, révèlent un rôle surprenant de la protéine tau dans la mémoire en lien avec la maladie d'Alzheimer, et proposent une protection accessible grâce au vaccin gratuit contre la méningite B proposé par le NHS aux nouveaux étudiants universitaires.
news_summary_es: Descubrimientos recientes revelan importantes vínculos entre la salud de las encías y los riesgos para las válvulas cardíacas, nuevos datos sobre la capacidad del cerebro para realizar varias tareas a la vez, un papel sorprendente de la proteína tau en la memoria relacionada con el Alzheimer, y una protección accesible gracias a la vacuna gratuita contra la meningitis B del NHS para los nuevos estudiantes universitarios.

articles.title: Scientists discover a hidden heart valve risk linked to gum disease
articles.summary: Researchers found that bacteria linked to gum disease may help drive the development of calcific aortic valve stenosis by triggering inflammation and calcium buildup in the heart valve. The early findings suggest that keeping gums healthy could play a role in reducing the risk of this serious heart condition.
articles.content:
Bacteria that cause gum disease may do more than damage your teeth. New preliminary research suggests they could also contribute to the buildup of calcium in the heart's aortic valve, potentially leading to calcific aortic valve stenosis (CAVS), a common and potentially life threatening heart valve disorder.

The findings were presented at the American Heart Association's Basic Cardiovascular Sciences Scientific Sessions 2026, held in Boston from July 13 to 16. The meeting is one of the world's largest gatherings focused on basic and translational cardiovascular research.

According to the American Heart Association, CAVS develops when the aortic valve gradually becomes thicker and more calcified, making it harder for blood to flow from the heart to the rest of the body. The condition often causes no symptoms at first. As it worsens, however, it can lead to fatigue, chest pain, shortness of breath, fainting, heart failure, and in some cases premature death. For people with severe disease, valve replacement surgery is currently the standard treatment.

The new research points to a possible biological mechanism connecting chronic gum disease and infection with the development of CAVS.

"There are currently no medications proven to prevent or slow the progression of CAVS. We hope our findings demonstrating the link between periodontal disease and CAVS will stimulate further research into new preventive and therapeutic approaches for this condition," said co lead author of the study, Chenyang Li, M.D., a Ph.D. candidate in the department of cardiology at the State Key Laboratory of Cardiovascular Disease of Fuwai Hospital's National Center for Cardiovascular Diseases, the Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences and Peking Union Medical College all in Beijing.

Researchers Focus on a Common Gum Disease Bacterium

The team concentrated on Porphyromonas gingivalis (P. gingivalis), a bacterium known to play a major role in periodontal disease by causing gum inflammation and the destruction of gum tissue. Previous research has also linked P. gingivalis to widespread inflammation throughout the body and an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, including artery plaque buildup and coronary artery disease.

To investigate the connection, researchers analyzed bacterial levels in heart valve tissue collected from people undergoing valve replacement surgery. They compared calcified aortic valves from people with CAVS to valve tissue from patients with other heart valve conditions.

"We were surprised by how much P. gingivalis was present in the calcified aortic valves," Li said. "Although it was not one of the most abundant bacteria overall, it showed one of the largest differences between valves with CAVS and valves without CAVS. This unexpected finding led us to investigate its potential role in the development of CAVS."

Mouse Study Reveals a Possible Disease Mechanism

The researchers then turned to mouse experiments to better understand how the bacterium might contribute to valve disease. Mice were exposed to either live or heat inactivated P. gingivalis to determine whether the bacteria accumulated in the aortic valve, increased calcium deposits, and produced symptoms consistent with aortic stenosis. Some animals also received preventive antibiotics, while others had the IL-1β inflammatory pathway genetically removed.

Repeated exposure to live P. gingivalis caused the bacteria to accumulate in the aortic valves of mice, leading to greater valve calcification and symptoms of aortic stenosis. Preventive antibiotic treatment reduced these effects. The researchers also found that P. gingivalis activated interleukin-1 beta (IL-1b), an inflammation promoting protein produced mainly by immune cells.

Additional experiments showed that genetically deleting IL-1b significantly reduced valve calcification and disease symptoms, even when P. gingivalis was present.

Good Oral Health May Benefit the Heart

"The key message is simple: take good care of your oral health," Li said. "Good oral hygiene and treatment of periodontal disease are important for overall health and may also have benefits for cardiovascular health. While it is still too early to recommend specific treatments for preventing CAVS, our findings suggest that periodontal health could be an important piece of the puzzle."

Eduardo Sanchez, M.D., M.P.H., FAHA, chief medical officer for prevention for the American Heart Association, said the findings add to growing evidence that oral health and heart health are closely connected.

"This study adds to the growing evidence that oral health and heart health are closely connected," said Eduardo Sanchez, M.D., M.P.H., FAHA, chief medical officer for prevention for the American Heart Association. "For many people, regular visits to the dentist are their only connection to the healthcare system. That makes dental professionals important partners in spotting health conditions, including periodontal disease early -- which can lead to quicker healthcare referrals and results, better health and lives saved."

The American Heart Association's Healthy Smiles, Healthy Hearts™ initiative supports dental professionals with educational resources about the connection between oral and cardiovascular health. The program also includes blood pressure screening guidance for dental practices and patient education materials designed to improve both oral health and heart health.

Early Findings Require Human Confirmation

The researchers emphasize that these results are preliminary and have not yet been confirmed in people. The work was presented as a scientific meeting abstract, meaning it has not yet undergone peer review or been published as a full journal paper.

To further investigate the possible connection between periodontal disease and CAVS, the research team has already launched a clinical study in people.

Research Highlights
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery linking gum disease bacteria to a serious heart valve condition, suggesting that maintaining oral health could reduce risk of calcific aortic valve stenosis. This finding has broad public health implications, offers a potential new preventive approach, and is supported by detailed research including human tissue analysis and animal studies. Although preliminary, it points to meaningful benefits for cardiovascular health and encourages further clinical research.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find link between gum disease bacteria and heart valve damage
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Gum disease is caused by bacteria that harm the gums and teeth. Scientists have long studied how this disease affects the body beyond the mouth.

**What happened** – Researchers discovered that a common gum disease bacterium called Porphyromonas gingivalis may contribute to a heart condition named calcific aortic valve stenosis (CAVS). This condition makes a heart valve stiff and narrow, blocking blood flow. The team studied heart valve tissue from patients and found more of this bacterium in damaged valves. In experiments with mice, the bacterium caused valve damage and calcium buildup. Antibiotics and blocking inflammation reduced the damage.

**Impact** – This is the first time a clear link has been found between gum disease bacteria and a serious heart valve problem. It suggests that keeping gums healthy might help lower the risk of CAVS, a condition that currently has no medicine to stop it and often needs surgery. It also shows how oral health can affect heart health in a new way.

**What's next step** – The research team has started a study in people to confirm these findings. They hope this will lead to new ways to prevent or treat CAVS by focusing on gum health. Meanwhile, experts encourage good dental care as part of overall health.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have found that bacteria from gum disease may cause heart valve damage, highlighting the importance of oral health for heart care.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent un lien entre la maladie des gencives et les dommages aux valves cardiaques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La maladie des gencives est causée par des bactéries qui attaquent les gencives et les dents. Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps comment cette maladie peut affecter d'autres parties du corps.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert qu'une bactérie courante de la maladie des gencives, appelée Porphyromonas gingivalis, pourrait contribuer à une maladie cardiaque appelée sténose aortique calcifiée (CAVS). Cette maladie rend une valve du cœur rigide et étroite, bloquant le flux sanguin. L'équipe a étudié des tissus de valves cardiaques et trouvé plus de cette bactérie dans les valves endommagées. Dans des expériences sur des souris, la bactérie a causé des dommages et des dépôts de calcium. Les antibiotiques et le blocage de l'inflammation ont réduit ces dommages.

**Impact** – C'est la première fois qu'un lien clair est trouvé entre une bactérie de la maladie des gencives et un grave problème de valve cardiaque. Cela suggère que garder les gencives en bonne santé pourrait aider à réduire le risque de CAVS, une maladie sans traitement médical actuel et souvent traitée par chirurgie. Cela montre aussi un nouveau lien entre la santé bucco-dentaire et la santé du cœur.

**Prochaine étape** – L'équipe de recherche a lancé une étude chez l'humain pour confirmer ces résultats. Ils espèrent que cela permettra de nouvelles façons de prévenir ou traiter la CAVS en se concentrant sur la santé des gencives. En attendant, les experts recommandent de bien prendre soin de ses dents.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont trouvé que des bactéries de la maladie des gencives peuvent endommager les valves du cœur, soulignant l'importance de la santé bucco-dentaire pour le cœur.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren vínculo entre bacterias de la enfermedad de las encías y daño en la válvula cardíaca
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La enfermedad de las encías es causada por bacterias que dañan las encías y los dientes. Los científicos han estudiado durante mucho tiempo cómo esta enfermedad afecta otras partes del cuerpo.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron que una bacteria común de la enfermedad de las encías llamada Porphyromonas gingivalis podría contribuir a una enfermedad del corazón llamada estenosis aórtica calcificada (CAVS). Esta enfermedad endurece y estrecha una válvula del corazón, dificultando el paso de la sangre. El equipo analizó tejidos de válvulas cardíacas y encontró más de esta bacteria en las válvulas dañadas. En experimentos con ratones, la bacteria causó daño y acumulación de calcio. Los antibióticos y bloquear la inflamación redujeron el daño.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se encuentra un vínculo claro entre una bacteria de la enfermedad de las encías y un problema grave en la válvula del corazón. Esto sugiere que mantener las encías saludables podría ayudar a reducir el riesgo de CAVS, una enfermedad que no tiene medicamento para detenerla y que a menudo requiere cirugía. También muestra una nueva conexión entre la salud bucal y la salud del corazón.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de investigación ha empezado un estudio en personas para confirmar estos hallazgos. Esperan que esto lleve a nuevas formas de prevenir o tratar la CAVS enfocándose en la salud de las encías. Mientras tanto, los expertos recomiendan cuidar bien los dientes.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Científicos encontraron que bacterias de la enfermedad de las encías pueden dañar las válvulas del corazón, destacando la importancia de la salud bucal para el cuidado del corazón.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a healthy human heart with a translucent aortic valve gently intertwined with stylized, glowing tooth roots and delicate bacteria shapes resembling Porphyromonas gingivalis nearby, connected by soft, flowing lines symbolizing the link between gum health and heart valve wellness, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and soft reds.

articles.title: Scientists discover how the brain rewires itself to truly multitask
articles.summary: Practice may do more than make perfect. Researchers found that extensive training physically reorganizes the brain, allowing learned tasks to bypass the prefrontal cortex and run through specialized circuits instead. By freeing the brain's "thinking" center, people became better at performing another task at the same time, challenging the long-held idea that humans only switch rapidly between tasks rather than truly multitask.
articles.content:
Researchers at Georgetown University have uncovered new evidence that the brain physically reorganizes itself as people master a skill, allowing well-practiced tasks to become automatic. The findings challenge the long-standing idea that humans cannot truly multitask, suggesting that with enough experience, the brain can perform certain activities simultaneously instead of simply switching rapidly between them.

The discovery could have implications beyond everyday life. It may help scientists better understand how habits form, why some behaviors are difficult to change, and how future artificial intelligence systems could become better at building new skills from previous learning.

"We have another stepping stone in our understanding of how the brain learns," said senior author Maximilian Riesenhuber, PhD, a professor of neuroscience at Georgetown University School of Medicine, and co-director of the Center for Neuroengineering. "The encouraging part is that you really can learn to multitask. There is actually a way to remodel your brain architecture and use other parts of your brain."

How the Brain Automates Learned Skills

The research expands on decades of work exploring how the brain acquires new abilities. While scientists have learned a great deal about the early stages of learning, much less has been known about what happens after a skill has been practiced extensively and becomes almost effortless.

Driving is a familiar example, Riesenhuber explained. Learning to drive initially demands constant attention, but years of experience allow many people to carry on a conversation, listen to music, or think through a problem while still driving safely.

"The question is: how does your brain do that?" Riesenhuber said.

Brain Scans Reveal a Shift in Neural Circuits

To investigate, the research team asked volunteers to sort morphed images of cars into two categories by identifying subtle visual differences. Participants completed more than 30,000 sorting trials over a period of 5 to 10 weeks using a smartphone app designed as a game.

The researchers examined participants' brains with fMRI and EEG scans before training began and again after the practice period ended.

Early in learning, the sorting task primarily activated the prefrontal cortex, the region responsible for executive functions such as planning, reasoning, and conscious decision-making. Because this part of the brain generally handles one demanding task at a time, it has long been viewed as a major limit on multitasking.

After weeks of practice, however, brain activity had shifted. The same categorization task was now being handled mainly by the temporal cortex, a region involved in memory and recognizing complex objects.

"Previous studies have shown that parts of the temporal cortex can be activated by particular object categories in experienced observers, birds, cars, even Pokémon, but a limitation of all of those studies is that they only looked after people became experts. The strength of this study is that it is longitudinal; we measure before and after training, so we can see that extensive training essentially put a category-selective area in the temporal lobe that was not there before," said first author Patrick Cox, PhD, who began the study as a graduate student in Riesenhuber's lab and is now an assistant professor of psychology at Lehigh University.

"This has implications for critical real-world scenarios, like when a radiologist can accurately classify masses on an X-ray as benign or malignant fairly automatically, often without extensive deliberation, thanks to years of training," Cox said.

How Brain Rewiring Enables Multitasking

The researchers found that information from the newly developed car selective area in the temporal cortex could bypass the prefrontal cortex and travel directly to brain regions responsible for producing responses.

"Experience remodels the brain to bypass that frontal bottleneck. The prefrontal cortex then stays free for whatever else you want to do, increasing your capacity," Riesenhuber explained.

The team also found that the more the car sorting task was "offloaded" from the prefrontal cortex, the better participants performed a second task at the same time.

That result challenges the long-accepted belief that people cannot truly multitask. Instead, many scientists have argued that the brain simply alternates attention between tasks so quickly that it creates the illusion of doing both at once.

"What we show is that the circuitry actually changes so the brain can do two things at once," Riesenhuber said. "This really is true multitasking."

What the Findings Mean for Habits and AI

The results may also provide new insight into compulsive behaviors. Because well-learned behaviors move into brain circuits that are less dependent on conscious control, simply trying to think about something else may not be enough to break an unwanted habit.

"The first step to unlearning something is understanding where it is actually happening in the brain," Riesenhuber said. "This shows why strategies like telling someone to think of something else don't really help, because they don't really have the behavior under conscious control."

The researchers also believe the findings may help explain why humans continue building new abilities throughout life while current AI systems still struggle to learn continuously without disrupting previously acquired knowledge.

According to Riesenhuber, transferring a well-learned skill into the temporal cortex frees the prefrontal cortex to focus on new challenges, allowing existing knowledge to serve as the foundation for future learning. Today's AI systems generally lack that kind of flexible architecture.

The team now plans to investigate exactly what signals move learning from one brain region to another and to determine which kinds of tasks can eventually be performed in parallel.

"Another really interesting question is what kinds of tasks can be learned well enough to do in parallel," Cox said. "We can walk and chew gum at the same time, but looking at our phones to text while driving will never be safe, because we take our eyes away from the road. It comes down to being able to train fully separate neural circuits for two tasks to become compatible."

The study, "Extensive Experience Remodels Neural Task Circuitry to Escape the Frontal Bottleneck and Increase Automaticity of Categorization," was published June 4 in the Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience.

In addition to Riesenhuber and Cox, the research team included Clara A. Scholl, Marissa L. Laws, Nelson E. Jaimes, and Xiong Jiang of Georgetown University. The work was supported by the National Science Foundation (BCS-1232530), the ARCS Foundation, and the Army Research Laboratory (W911NF-24-1-0097). The authors reported no personal financial interests related to the study.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant neuroscience discovery showing how extensive training physically rewires the brain to enable true multitasking by offloading tasks from the prefrontal cortex to specialized circuits. This finding has broad implications for understanding brain function, habit formation, and improving learning and AI systems, offering tangible benefits to society's knowledge of human cognition and potential applications in health and education.
articles.personality_title: Scientists find how the brain rewires itself to truly multitask
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context**
Scientists have long believed that people cannot really multitask. Instead, the brain quickly switches attention between tasks. This is because the prefrontal cortex, the brain’s thinking center, can only handle one difficult task at a time.

**What happened**
Researchers at Georgetown University trained volunteers to sort images of cars over several weeks using a smartphone game. They scanned the volunteers’ brains before and after this training. At first, the prefrontal cortex was active during the task. After practice, the brain shifted the work to the temporal cortex, a different area linked to memory and recognizing objects. This change allowed the brain to bypass the prefrontal cortex and handle the task automatically.

**Impact**
Because the prefrontal cortex was freed up, people could perform another task at the same time, showing true multitasking. This discovery changes how scientists understand learning and brain function. It also helps explain why habits are hard to break—because they become automatic in different brain circuits. Furthermore, this finding could guide improvements in artificial intelligence by showing how skills can build on each other without interference.

**What's next step**
The researchers want to find out exactly how the brain moves learning from one area to another. They also plan to study which tasks can be learned well enough to be done at the same time safely, like walking and chewing gum, compared to unsafe multitasking like texting while driving.

**One-sentence takeaway**
With enough practice, the brain rewires itself to perform some tasks automatically, allowing true multitasking by using different brain circuits.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent comment le cerveau se réorganise pour véritablement faire plusieurs tâches à la fois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte**
Les scientifiques pensaient depuis longtemps que les humains ne pouvaient pas vraiment faire plusieurs tâches à la fois. Le cerveau bascule rapidement son attention d’une tâche à l’autre car le cortex préfrontal, le centre de la réflexion, ne peut gérer qu’une tâche difficile à la fois.

**Ce qui s’est passé**
Des chercheurs de l’université de Georgetown ont entraîné des volontaires à trier des images de voitures pendant plusieurs semaines grâce à un jeu sur smartphone. Ils ont scanné leur cerveau avant et après cet entraînement. Au début, le cortex préfrontal était actif pendant la tâche. Après l’entraînement, le travail a été transféré au cortex temporal, une autre zone liée à la mémoire et à la reconnaissance d’objets. Ce changement a permis au cerveau de contourner le cortex préfrontal et d’automatiser la tâche.

**Impact**
Libéré du cortex préfrontal, le cerveau peut alors faire une autre tâche en même temps, montrant un vrai multitâche. Cette découverte change la compréhension de l’apprentissage et du fonctionnement cérébral. Elle explique aussi pourquoi les habitudes sont difficiles à changer, car elles deviennent automatiques dans d’autres circuits cérébraux. Enfin, cela pourrait aider à améliorer l’intelligence artificielle en montrant comment les compétences peuvent s’appuyer les unes sur les autres sans se gêner.

**Prochaine étape**
Les chercheurs veulent comprendre comment le cerveau déplace l’apprentissage d’une zone à une autre. Ils souhaitent aussi étudier quelles tâches peuvent être suffisamment bien apprises pour être réalisées en parallèle en toute sécurité, comme marcher et mâcher du chewing-gum, par opposition à des multitâches dangereux comme envoyer des textos en conduisant.

**Résumé en une phrase**
Avec suffisamment d’entraînement, le cerveau se réorganise pour automatiser certaines tâches, permettant un vrai multitâche grâce à différentes voies cérébrales.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren cómo el cerebro se reconfigura para hacer multitareas reales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto**
Durante mucho tiempo, los científicos creyeron que las personas no pueden hacer varias tareas al mismo tiempo. El cerebro cambia rápidamente su atención de una tarea a otra porque la corteza prefrontal, el centro de pensamiento, solo puede manejar una tarea difícil a la vez.

**Qué pasó**
Investigadores de la Universidad de Georgetown entrenaron a voluntarios para clasificar imágenes de autos durante varias semanas usando un juego en el teléfono. Escanearon sus cerebros antes y después del entrenamiento. Al principio, la corteza prefrontal estaba activa durante la tarea. Después, el trabajo pasó a la corteza temporal, una zona vinculada a la memoria y reconocimiento de objetos. Este cambio permitió que el cerebro evitara la corteza prefrontal y automatizara la tarea.

**Impacto**
Al liberar la corteza prefrontal, el cerebro pudo hacer otra tarea al mismo tiempo, mostrando un verdadero multitasking. Este hallazgo cambia la forma en que entendemos el aprendizaje y el funcionamiento cerebral. También explica por qué los hábitos son difíciles de cambiar, porque se vuelven automáticos en diferentes circuitos cerebrales. Además, podría ayudar a mejorar la inteligencia artificial al mostrar cómo las habilidades pueden construirse unas sobre otras sin interferencias.

**Próximo paso**
Los investigadores quieren descubrir cómo el cerebro mueve el aprendizaje de una zona a otra. También planean estudiar qué tareas se pueden aprender lo suficientemente bien para hacerlas en paralelo de forma segura, como caminar y mascar chicle, en comparación con multitareas peligrosas como enviar mensajes mientras se conduce.

**Resumen en una frase**
Con suficiente práctica, el cerebro se reconfigura para realizar algunas tareas automáticamente, permitiendo un verdadero multitasking usando diferentes circuitos cerebrales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a glowing human brain composed of interconnected puzzle pieces shifting and rearranging, with one side showing a complex network of bright pathways symbolizing the prefrontal cortex gradually transforming into a smoother, flowing pattern on the temporal cortex side, illuminated softly by natural earth tones and subtle light to represent the brain rewiring itself for effortless multitasking and skill mastery.

articles.title: Alzheimer's tau protein has a surprising secret role in memory
articles.summary: Researchers found that tau is essential for turning new experiences into lasting memories by helping organize the brain's memory-storing cells. The mouse study also revealed how abnormal tau may contribute to Alzheimer's by disrupting both the formation of new memories and the recall of existing ones.
articles.content:
New research has revealed that tau, a protein best known for its connection to Alzheimer's disease, is also essential for creating long lasting memories. The discovery provides new insight into how healthy memory works and could help guide future efforts to develop treatments for dementia.

The study, led by Flinders University in partnership with researchers from the University of New South Wales and Macquarie University, was published in Nature Communications. It found that tau helps organize and stabilize memories so they can be retained over time.

The researchers studied "remote memory" in mice, which refers to memories recalled days or weeks after an experience. They discovered that tau is not necessary for learning something new or remembering it shortly afterward. Instead, it plays a crucial role in making those memories durable over the long term.

Because the research was conducted in mice, the findings cannot be directly applied to human memory or Alzheimer's disease. Even so, the results offer valuable clues that could shape future dementia research and treatment strategies.

Tau's Role in Long Lasting Memory

Senior author Associate Professor Arne Ittner, a neuroscientist from Flinders' College of Medicine and Public Health, says the findings help explain why people with dementia may still be able to learn new information initially, yet struggle to retain it.

"Why some memories last while others fade has long puzzled scientists and our study shows that tau plays a key role in how the brain forms long-lasting memories. Without it, memories can still form in the moment, but they are weaker," says Associate Professor Ittner.

The team focused on specialized brain cells called "engram cells," which create the physical record of a memory. When a new experience occurs, only a small number of these cells are selected to store it.

According to the study, tau is active during this critical stage of memory formation, helping determine exactly which engram cells are recruited to preserve the experience.

One of the study's lead authors, Renée Kosonen, says tau acts like an organizer that helps the brain build accurate and lasting memories.

"Our findings show that tau helps determine which cells are selected to store a memory, shaping how an experience forms a lasting memory trace," says Ms Kosonen, a researcher at Flinders' Neuroscience and Dementia Research.

How Tau Organizes Memory

The researchers also found that tau reduces unnecessary or "noise" activity in the brain during memory formation. By limiting this background activity, tau allows only a specific group of cells to become part of a memory, producing clearer and more stable memory traces.

The team identified an important molecular process behind this effect. As learning takes place, tau undergoes a subtle chemical change called phosphorylation, which helps coordinate the activity of engram cells.

Although abnormal tau phosphorylation is a well known feature of Alzheimer's disease, the study shows that controlled, low level phosphorylation is a normal and essential part of healthy brain function.

New Clues About Alzheimer's Disease

The researchers made another surprising discovery. Even in the absence of tau, memory traces still existed and could be recovered by directly stimulating engram cells. This suggests that tau is not required to store memories themselves. Instead, it appears to be needed to connect natural cues, such as sights and sounds, with the ability to recall those memories.

The findings also provide new insight into how Alzheimer's related tau may interfere with memory. When disease associated forms of tau were present in engram cells during learning, they disrupted the creation of new memories. When those abnormal forms appeared after memories had already formed, they interfered with the brain's ability to retrieve them.

These effects were associated with abnormal patterns of brain activity, suggesting that memory problems in dementia may result not only from memories being lost, but also from disruptions in how memories are organized and accessed.

"Knowing how tau supports the formation and recall of memory could help us better understand what goes wrong in memory loss," says Associate Professor Ittner.

"Future research will hopefully be able to confirm concepts developed in our study in human memory and show their implication in dementia."

The researchers conclude that tau should be viewed not only as a protein involved in Alzheimer's disease, but also as a fundamental regulator of how the brain organizes, stores, and retrieves lasting memories. That new perspective could deepen scientists' understanding of both healthy memory and the biological changes that contribute to Alzheimer's disease.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific discovery about tau protein's essential role in memory formation and its implications for understanding and potentially treating Alzheimer's disease. The research offers meaningful insights that could influence future dementia therapies, benefiting a broad population affected by memory disorders. The study is detailed and focused on a single important topic with clear real-world health impact.
articles.personality_title: New study reveals tau protein’s key role in forming lasting memories
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long known that tau protein is linked to Alzheimer’s disease, a condition that causes memory loss. However, its role in healthy memory was unclear.

**What happened** – Researchers from Flinders University and other Australian institutions studied mice and found that tau helps the brain turn new experiences into long-lasting memories. Tau organizes special brain cells called engram cells, which store memories over time. This protein is not needed to learn or remember something right away but is crucial for keeping memories strong days or weeks later.

**Impact** – This discovery explains why people with dementia can learn new things but have trouble remembering them later. Tau acts like a memory organizer, helping the brain select the right cells to store clear and stable memories. It also reduces background brain activity to make memory traces clearer. The study offers new ideas about how abnormal tau in Alzheimer’s disrupts memory formation and recall.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope to confirm these findings in humans and explore how tau’s role in memory can guide better treatments for dementia. Understanding tau’s normal function may help scientists find ways to protect or restore memory in diseases like Alzheimer’s.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Tau protein plays a vital role in turning new experiences into lasting memories by organizing brain cells, offering fresh insight into memory and Alzheimer’s disease.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude révèle le rôle clé de la protéine tau dans la formation des souvenirs durables
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – On sait depuis longtemps que la protéine tau est liée à la maladie d’Alzheimer, une maladie qui cause des pertes de mémoire. Cependant, son rôle dans la mémoire saine restait inconnu.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université Flinders et d’autres institutions australiennes ont étudié des souris et découvert que la protéine tau aide le cerveau à transformer de nouvelles expériences en souvenirs durables. Tau organise des cellules cérébrales spéciales appelées cellules engrammes, qui stockent les souvenirs sur le long terme. Cette protéine n’est pas nécessaire pour apprendre ou se souvenir immédiatement, mais elle est essentielle pour conserver les souvenirs plusieurs jours ou semaines plus tard.

**Impact** – Cette découverte explique pourquoi les personnes atteintes de démence peuvent apprendre de nouvelles choses mais ont du mal à s’en souvenir ensuite. Tau agit comme un organisateur de la mémoire, aidant le cerveau à choisir les bonnes cellules pour stocker des souvenirs clairs et stables. Elle réduit aussi l’activité cérébrale de fond pour rendre les traces de mémoire plus nettes. L’étude apporte de nouvelles idées sur la façon dont la tau anormale dans Alzheimer perturbe la formation et le rappel des souvenirs.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent confirmer ces résultats chez l’humain et explorer comment le rôle de tau dans la mémoire peut guider de meilleurs traitements contre la démence. Comprendre la fonction normale de tau pourrait aider à protéger ou restaurer la mémoire dans des maladies comme Alzheimer.

**Une phrase clé** – La protéine tau joue un rôle essentiel pour transformer les nouvelles expériences en souvenirs durables en organisant les cellules cérébrales, offrant un nouvel éclairage sur la mémoire et la maladie d’Alzheimer.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio revela el papel clave de la proteína tau en la formación de recuerdos duraderos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos saben desde hace tiempo que la proteína tau está relacionada con la enfermedad de Alzheimer, una condición que causa pérdida de memoria. Sin embargo, su papel en la memoria saludable no estaba claro.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad Flinders y otras instituciones australianas estudiaron ratones y descubrieron que la proteína tau ayuda al cerebro a convertir nuevas experiencias en recuerdos duraderos. Tau organiza células cerebrales especiales llamadas células engramas, que almacenan recuerdos a lo largo del tiempo. Esta proteína no es necesaria para aprender o recordar algo de inmediato, pero es crucial para mantener fuertes los recuerdos días o semanas después.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento explica por qué las personas con demencia pueden aprender cosas nuevas pero tienen problemas para recordarlas después. Tau actúa como un organizador de la memoria, ayudando al cerebro a seleccionar las células correctas para almacenar recuerdos claros y estables. También reduce la actividad cerebral de fondo para hacer que las huellas de la memoria sean más claras. El estudio ofrece nuevas ideas sobre cómo la tau anormal en el Alzheimer interfiere con la formación y recuperación de recuerdos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan confirmar estos hallazgos en humanos y explorar cómo el papel de tau en la memoria puede guiar mejores tratamientos para la demencia. Entender la función normal de tau podría ayudar a proteger o restaurar la memoria en enfermedades como el Alzheimer.

**Una frase clave** – La proteína tau juega un papel vital para convertir nuevas experiencias en recuerdos duraderos al organizar las células cerebrales, ofreciendo una nueva visión sobre la memoria y el Alzheimer.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing network of interconnected brain cells shaped like delicate threads of tau protein gently organizing and linking clusters of glowing memory "engrams" represented as small, shimmering orbs, set against a soft, natural background of muted earth tones and pale blues, symbolizing the protein’s essential role in stabilizing and preserving long-lasting memories.

articles.title: Starting uni? What to know about having the free NHS meningitis B jab
articles.summary: It follows the UK's largest and fastest growing meningitis B outbreak to date in Kent earlier this year.
articles.content:
Bookings can be made at high street pharmacies across England from Monday 13 July, with the first appointments available from Monday 20 July.

Seventeen and 18-year-olds can book via the NHS National Booking Service, external if they are already registered with a GP, external.

Other students under the age of 25 who are heading to a residential college or university this autumn for the first time can also contact a participating pharmacy.

You can get a walk-in MenB vaccine at a pharmacy if you were born on or after 21 July 2001, and for the first time this autumn you are:

starting university

starting at a further education college as a residential student

International students and students from elsewhere in the UK (Scotland, Wales, Northern Ireland, the Channel Islands, the Isle of Man) who will be studying in England are also eligible, as well as those who are travelling abroad to study.

You do not need to be registered with a GP surgery to use the walk-in service.

Find a participating pharmacy here, external.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the availability of free NHS meningitis B vaccinations for students starting university or residential colleges, addressing a significant public health concern following the UK's largest meningitis B outbreak. This initiative has broad positive impact by helping prevent disease among a large, vulnerable population, with clear details on eligibility and access.
articles.personality_title: Free meningitis B vaccine now available for students starting university
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Earlier this year, Kent experienced the UK’s largest outbreak of meningitis B, a serious infection that can affect the brain and spinal cord. To protect young people, the NHS is offering free vaccinations to students starting university or residential colleges this autumn.

**What happened** – From July 13, students aged 17 to 18 can book their free meningitis B vaccine at high street pharmacies across England. The first appointments begin on July 20. Other students under 25 starting university or residential college for the first time can also get vaccinated at participating pharmacies without needing to register with a doctor.

**Impact** – This vaccination program targets a group at higher risk of meningitis B, especially those living away from home for the first time. By making the vaccine free and easy to access, including for international and UK students studying in England, the NHS helps reduce the chance of new outbreaks and protects many young people at an important time in their lives.

**What's next step** – Students should book or visit a pharmacy soon to get vaccinated before starting their studies. The NHS will continue monitoring the situation to keep students safe and may expand the program if needed.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The NHS is offering a free meningitis B vaccine at pharmacies to protect new university and college students from a serious infection following a recent outbreak.
articles.personality_title_fr: Vaccin gratuit contre la méningite B maintenant disponible pour les étudiants entrant à l'université
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Plus tôt cette année, le Kent a connu la plus grande épidémie de méningite B au Royaume-Uni, une infection grave qui peut toucher le cerveau et la moelle épinière. Pour protéger les jeunes, le NHS propose des vaccinations gratuites aux étudiants entrant à l'université ou en internat cet automne.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – À partir du 13 juillet, les étudiants de 17 à 18 ans peuvent prendre rendez-vous pour leur vaccin gratuit contre la méningite B dans les pharmacies de rue en Angleterre. Les premiers rendez-vous sont disponibles dès le 20 juillet. D'autres étudiants de moins de 25 ans entrant à l'université ou en internat pour la première fois peuvent aussi se faire vacciner dans les pharmacies participantes sans avoir besoin d'être inscrits chez un médecin.

**Impact** – Ce programme de vaccination cible un groupe à risque plus élevé de méningite B, surtout ceux qui vivent loin de chez eux pour la première fois. En rendant le vaccin gratuit et facile d'accès, y compris pour les étudiants internationaux et britanniques étudiant en Angleterre, le NHS aide à réduire le risque de nouvelles épidémies et protège de nombreux jeunes à un moment important de leur vie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les étudiants doivent prendre rendez-vous ou se rendre rapidement en pharmacie pour se faire vacciner avant de commencer leurs études. Le NHS continuera de surveiller la situation pour assurer la sécurité des étudiants et pourrait élargir le programme si nécessaire.

**En une phrase** – Le NHS offre un vaccin gratuit contre la méningite B en pharmacie pour protéger les nouveaux étudiants universitaires et en internat d'une infection grave après une récente épidémie.
articles.personality_title_es: Vacuna gratuita contra la meningitis B disponible para estudiantes que comienzan la universidad
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A principios de este año, Kent tuvo el brote más grande de meningitis B en el Reino Unido, una infección grave que puede afectar el cerebro y la médula espinal. Para proteger a los jóvenes, el NHS ofrece vacunas gratuitas a estudiantes que comienzan la universidad o colegios residenciales este otoño.

**Qué pasó** – Desde el 13 de julio, los estudiantes de 17 y 18 años pueden reservar su vacuna gratuita contra la meningitis B en farmacias de Inglaterra. Las primeras citas están disponibles desde el 20 de julio. Otros estudiantes menores de 25 años que comienzan la universidad o un colegio residencial por primera vez también pueden vacunarse en farmacias participantes sin necesidad de estar registrados con un médico.

**Impacto** – Este programa de vacunación se enfoca en un grupo con mayor riesgo de meningitis B, especialmente quienes viven fuera de casa por primera vez. Al ofrecer la vacuna gratis y de fácil acceso, incluyendo a estudiantes internacionales y del Reino Unido que estudian en Inglaterra, el NHS ayuda a reducir nuevas epidemias y protege a muchos jóvenes en un momento importante de sus vidas.

**Próximo paso** – Los estudiantes deben reservar o acudir pronto a una farmacia para vacunarse antes de comenzar sus estudios. El NHS seguirá vigilando la situación para mantener la seguridad y podría ampliar el programa si es necesario.

**Una frase clave** – El NHS ofrece la vacuna gratuita contra la meningitis B en farmacias para proteger a los nuevos estudiantes universitarios y de colegios residenciales tras un reciente brote.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a welcoming community pharmacy storefront with a softly glowing NHS sign, surrounded by symbolic elements like a protective shield shaped subtly like a vaccine vial and a group of diverse, abstract student silhouettes carrying backpacks, all rendered in gentle, natural tones of blues, greens, and warm neutrals.
